text: BJORNSTJERNA BAY, an inlet on the W shore of the gulf of Boothia, in N lat. 71° 10'.
BJORSATER. See BIORSATER.
BJURFORS, a village of Sweden, in the prov. of Westeraas, 23 m. from Avesta. Pop. 300. There is a brass-foundry here.
BLAAVAND, a town of Denmark, in Jutland, in the bailiwick of Ribe, on the Blaavand's Huk, at the entrance of the Elbe.
BLABY, a parish in Leicestershire, 4 m. S by W of Leicester. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 1,896.
BLACE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Villefranche. Pop. 1,028.
BLACHERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Joyeuse. Pop. 2,865.
BLACK, a township in Posey co., in the state of Iowa, U. S. Pop. 2,526.

BLACKADDER, a stream in the Merse division of Berwickshire, which joins the Whitadder above Allanton, after a course of 20 m. It rises in the NW part of Westruther p., at an alt. of 1,130 ft. above sea-level.
BLACKAUTON, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. W of Dartmouth. Area 5,850 acres. Pop. 1,449.

BLACK BAY, a bay on the SE coast of Labrador, 11 m. W of Red bay. It is 3 m. wide, and 2 m. deep. -Also a bay on the N shore of Lake Superior. It is formed by bold precipitous rocks, which are so powerfully magnetic, that at its entrance the needle loses its power entirely, and, in sailing from one headland to another, gradually changes its direction three or four points as it recedes from one group of joyed the advantages of this branch of the cotton rocks and approaches another.—Also a bay on the coast of Virginia.

BLACK BEAR ISLAND (LAKE), a lake of Upper Canada, formed by an expansion of the Missinippi. It is 22 m. in length, and varies from 3 to 5 m. in breadth, yet is so choked with islands, that no channel exceeding a mile in breadth is found in it.

BLACK BEARD'S ISLAND, an island off the Lancashire. The cotton manufactures of B. at pre- BLA coast of Georgia, U.S., containing 1,600 acres. It is the property of the general government, having been purchased, in 1800, on account of its valuable oak timber, for 15,000 dollars. Most of the wood has been since cut down.
BLACKBOROUGH, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. ESE of Collumpton. Pop. returned with Kentisbere.
BLACKBOURTON, a parish in Oxfordshire, 6 m. SSE of Burford. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 331.
BLACK BROOK, a township in Clinton co., in the state of New York, U. S., 20 m. SW of Plattsburg. Pop. 1,064.
BLACKBULL, a village in co. Meath, 10 m. NW of Dublin.

BLACKBURN, a parish and market-town in the co.-palatine of Lancaster, 212 m. NW of London; 30 m. SE of Lancaster; and 24 m. NNW of Manchester. The p. comprises 23 townships, viz., the market-town of B., and the townships of Balderston, Billington, Lango, Great Harward, Over Darwen, Salisbury, Samlesbury, Tockholes, Walton-le-dale, Clayton-ledale, Cuerdale, Lower Darwen, Dinkley, Eccleshill, Little Harwood, Livesley, Mellor, Osbaldeston, Pleasington, Ramsgrave, Richton, Wilpshire, and Witton. These townships, except such as are under the immediate care of the vicar of B., are divided into 8 chapelries. The p., which is 14 m. long, by 10 m. broad, was originally part of the p. of Whalley. Its area is 45,620 acres. Pop. in 1790, 5,000; in 1831, 59,791; in 1841, 71,711. The district comprises some of the most extensive cotton-mills, calico-printing works, and other manufacturing establishments in Lancashire. The town of B., formerly the cap. of a sterile district called Blackburnshire, stands on a small stream, anciently called Blakeburn, or 'the Yellow stream;' and is sheltered by hills on the NE and NW. The streets are irregularly built, but contain some good houses. A theatre was erected here in 1818, and there are assembly-rooms and a subscription library. The church, originally part of the ancient monastery of Whalley, was almost entirely rebuilt in 1819. In 1826, a new church was completed at an expense of £26,000. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the various branches of the cotton-manufacture. In 1821, of 9,795 families in this parish, only 552 were employed in agriculture. In 1838 there were 44 cotton-mills, employing 14,460 hands. The manufactures of B. appear to have arisen in the time of the Commonwealth. The first fabrics for which this place was distinguished, were called 'B. checks,' a species of cloth consisting of a linen warp and cotton woof, one or both of which being dyed in the thread, gave to the piece, when woven, a striped or checked appearance. This article was afterwards superseded by the 'B. greys,' so called from the colour, neither the warp nor the weft having been dyed. The component parts of this cloth consisted of a mixture of linen and cotton, and when manufactured, the pieces were generally sent to London to be printed. The great era of improvements in the cotton business which opened about 1765, led the manufacturers of this district to turn their attention to the making of calicoes,—so called from their resemblance to the India manufacture brought from the province of Calicut,—and from that time to the present B. has enbusiness more perhaps than any other place in Lancashire. James Hargrave, a carpenter of B., in 1767, constructed an engine that would at once spin 20 or 30 threads of cotton into yarn for the fustian manufacture; but because it was likely to answer in some measure the end proposed, his engines were burnt and destroyed by a mob, and himself driven out of sent employ 1,800 horse-power. The number of pieces of cotton-goods manufactured at B. weekly is now estimated at 49,200, the workmanship of which, in the various processes, gives employment to 10,000 persons; and the annual value of these goods before they are dyed and printed, is calculated at £2,000,000 sterling. One main cause of the extent of the cotton manufacture here is to be found in the abundant supply of fuel furnished by the S part of this parish. —The town is governed by commissioners, appointed according to act of parliament. Under the Reform act it returns two members. The pop. of the parliamentary borough in 1841 was 36,186. Number of electors in 1842-3, 910. Assessed property in 1815, £37,758; in 1842-3, £95,474. The town is <math>29\frac{1}{4}</math> m. from Manchester by the Manchester and Bury railway; and 20 m. from Settle. By the Preston and Accrington line, it is 121 m. from Preston.

BLACKBURN, a village in the parishes of Livingstone and Whitburn, in Linlithgowshire, 31 m. W of Livingstone. Pop. 443. It is near a stream of the same name, the principal branch of the Linlithgowshire Almond.
BLACKBURN, a small stream of Liddesdale, in Roxburghshire, an affluent of the Liddel, celebrated for its romantic scenery and cascades.
BLACK (CAPE), or RAS KA'U, a projecting headland on the S coast of Arabia, in N lat. 12° 39′ 45″, E long. 44° 32½'.
BLACK CAVE HEAD, a basaltic headland on the coast of co. Antrim, in N lat. 54° 54'.
BLACK COMB, a mountain on the coast of Cumberland, at the mouth of the Duddon, opposite the isle of Man. Alt. 1,919 ft.

BLACK CREEK, a river in Alleghany co., in the state of New York, U.S., which runs NE into the Genessee river.—Also a river of New Jersey, which runs into the Delaware.—Also a river of Georgia, which runs into the Savannah.—Also a river of Virginia, which runs into York river.—Also a river in the state of Mississippi, which joins the Gazoo.—Also a river of South Carolina, which runs into the Pedee.

BLACK CREEK, a village in Alleghany co., in the state of New York, U. S., 275 m. W by S of Albany.—Also a township in Wayne co., in N Carolina, 73 m. SE of Raleigh.—Also a township in Scriven co., in Georgia, 128 m. E by S of Milledgeville.—Also a township of Sullivan co., in Iowa, 96 m. SW of Indianapolis.
BLACKDEN, a township in the p. of Sandbach, Cheshire, 6 m. SSE of Nether Knutsford. Area 760 acres. Pop. 266.
BLACK DOWN, a point of the South Downs, or Chalk-hill range, near Beaminster in Dorsetshire. Alt. 817 ft.
BLACKFORD, a parish in Somersetshire, 5 m. WSW of Wincanton. Area 680 acres. Pop. 178.— Also a p. in Perthshire, on the N flank of the Ochills. Pop. 1,782. The Ruthven and the Allen rise in this p.
BLACKFORD, a county in the NE part of the state of Iowa, U. S., watered by the Salamanie river. Area 182 sq. m. Pop. 1,226. Its cap., of the same name, is 81 m. NE of Indianapolis.
BLACKFORDBY, a chapelry in the p. of Seal, in Leicestershire, 2 m. WNW of Ashby-de-la-Zouch. Pop. 478.
BLACK FOREST. See Schwarzwald.
BLACK FOOT INDIANS, one of the most powerful aboriginal tribes in the NW region of America. They still number 30,000 souls, and consist of three divisions, of which the Cotomay is best known.
BLACKHALL, a headland in co. Cork, on the N side of the entrance of Bantry bay, 41 m W by S of Bere island.
BLACK HAWK, a village in Shelby co., in the place. BLA state of Iowa, U.S., 34 m. SE of Indianapolis. Pop. 102.

BLACK HEAD, a promontory in co. Antrim, on the N side of the extreme entrance of Belfast Lough, 13 m. SE of the head of Lough Larne.—Also a promontory in co. Cork, projecting boldly from the E side of the peninsula which separates the bay of Kinsale from that of Courtmacsherry, 2 m. N by E of the Old Head of Kinsale.—Also a cape in co. Clare, forming the extremity of an angular projection of the county into Galway bay, 10 m. SW by W of Galway, and 93 m. ENE of the nearest of the Arran islands. -The spacious marine inlet on the W, entering between it and Finvarra point, is sometimes called Blackhead bay.—Also a headland on the coast of Cornwall, 24 m. NW by W of the Eddystone light. -Also a cape on the coast of Wigtonshire, 6 m. WSW of Stranraer.—Also a cape on the E coast of New Zealand, 25 m. NNE of Cape Kidnappen.

BLACKHEATH, a hamlet in the p. of Greenwich, 61 m. SE of London. There are numerous handsome villas here on the heath as it is called, and a college or hospital for the support of "poor merchants who have lost their fortunes by accidents on the sea," founded in 1695. The heath has been the scene of several memorable transactions in English history.
BLACKHEDDON, a township in the p. of Stamfordham, in Northumberland, 14 m. NW of Newcastle. Pop. 73.

BLACK LAKE, one of a chain of lakes in the Athabasca district, in British N America, 330 m. W of Fort Churchhill, in N lat. 59°, W long. 106°.- Also a lake in Upper Canada, in N lat. 53° 50', communicating with Severn lake by the river Severn.- Also a lake in the co. of St. Lawrence, in the state of New York, U. S., formed by an expansion of Indian river. It is 20 m. long, and 2 m. in average breadth.
BLACKLAND, a parish in Wilts, 12 m. SE of Calne. Area 460 acres. Pop. 81.—Also a tything in Calne p., Wilts. Pop. 73.
BLACKLEY, a chapelry in the p. and 3 m. N of Manchester, in Lancashire, on the Irk. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 3,302.
BLACKLEYSVILLE, a village in Wayne co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 94 m. NE of Columbus.
BLACKLION, a village in co. Meath, 5 m. S of Slane.
BLACKMANN'S BAY, a small bay on the W side of the estuary of the Derwent in Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 42° 7'.
BLACKMORE, a parish in Essex, 3½ m. NW by W of Ingatestone. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 709.— Also a tything in the p. of Melksham, Wilts. Pop. 279.
BLACK MOUNTAINS. See KARABAGH.

BLACKNESS, a small port on the frith of Forth, in Linlithgowshire, 5 m. W of Queensferry. An old castle here is supposed to mark the E extremity of Antoninus's wall. The port was at one time of considerable importance; and the castle is one of those fortified places which were to be kept up by government according to the articles of union of the two kingdoms.
BLACK NIB, a headland on the E coast of co. Down, 13 m. N by E of the entrance of Lough Strangford.
BLACK POINT, a headland on the coast of Carnarvonshire, at the NE entrance to the Menai strait. -Also a headland on the coast of Labrador, in N lat. 59° 20'.
BLACKPOOL, a chapelry in the p. of Bispham, in Lancashire, 4 m. SW of Poulton, on the coast. The village was formerly an inconsiderable hamlet, but is now much resorted to in summer as a bathing-
BLACKRATH, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 21 m. NE of Kilkenny. Area 1,759 acres. Pop. 341.

BLACK RIVER, a river in Elizabeth p., in the island of Jamaica, flowing through a flat country, and navigable for boats 30 m.—Also a river on the Mosquito coast, which flows into the gulf of Mexico, 8 m. from Cape Camaron, near a mountain called the Sugar-loaf, 2,050 ft. in alt.—Also a river in Windsor co., in the state of Vermont, U.S., 35 m. long, which enters Connecticut river at Springfield.—Also a large river in the State of New York, which rises in Herkimer co., and, after a course of 108 m., falls into a bay at the lower end of Lake Ontario. At Leyden, this river falls 63 ft., and becomes navigable for boats to Carthage, a distance of 40 m. Below Carthage, it becomes unnavigable from rapids. A canal, 35 m. in length, is executing from Leyden falls to the Erie canal at Rome.—Also a river in North Carolina, one of the E branches of Cape Fear river.—Also a river in Arkansas, which enters White river, 80 m. from the N boundary of Arkansas.—Also a river in Michigan, which flows into Lake Michigan after a course of 18 m., and expands into Black lake, 5 m. above its mouth.

BLACK RIVER, a township in Lorain co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 130 m. NNE of Columbus, at the mouth of a river of the same name on Lake Erie. It has a good harbour. Pop. 668.—Also a village in St. Francis co., in Missouri. Pop. 101.— Also a township in Washington co., Missouri. Pop. 685.—Also a township in Wayne co., Missouri. Pop. 528.—Also a township in Independence co., Arkansas. Pop. 476.—Also a township in Lawrence co., Arkansas. Pop. 100.

BLACK ROCK, a rock or islet 4 m. SW by W of Carnsore point, in co. Wexford,—Also a rock on the N side, and near the entrance of Sligo bay, in co. Sligo.—Also a small creek on the N side of Galway bay, 3 m. W of Galway, in co. Galway.—Also a v. in the p. of Haggardstown, co. Louth, at the head of a small bay, 23 m. SE of Dundalk. Pop. 507.—Also a town in the p. of Monkstown, in co. Dublin, on the S shore of Dublin bay, 4 m. SE of Dublin castle. Pop. 2,372. It has long been a great resort of the Dublin citizens for suburban rustication; and is in fact a mere expansion of the almost continued stream of town which extends along the whole S margin of the bay.—Also a v. in the p. of St. Finbar, in co. Cork, on the S shore of the incipient estuary of the Lee, forming one of the finest sections of the gorgeous environs of that city. Pop. 303.

BLACK ROCK, a township and village in Erie co., in the state of New York, U.S. The township extends on 3 sides of Buffalo, from which it is centrally distant 3 m. Pop. 3,625. Squaw and Strawberry islands, each containing about 100 acres, belong to this township.—The v. is 2\frac{1}{2} m. below Buffalo, of which it may be considered as a suburb, and with which it is connected by a railroad 3 m. in length. It is 290 m. from Albany by land; and 316 m. by the Erie canal. The harbour is formed by an immense wood and stone pier projecting 1,500 ft. into the Niagara river; built by the state of New York for the double purpose of forming a safe and convenient harbour for vessels on the lake, and for canal boats here entering the lake; and also for securing to the Erie canal a proper supply of water directly from the lake. The pier operates as a breakwater, and by confining the creek has served to clear its channel, so that vessels drawing 8 ft. water pass freely. The greatest difficulty to be encountered in this harbour arises from the accumulation of ice, which, on the breaking up of the lake, is often driven into it by W winds. There is a ferry here across the lake to Waterloo in Canada. BLA
BLACKROD, a chapelry and v. in the p. of Bol ton, in Lancashire, 43 m. SSE of Chorley. Area of c. 2,660 acres. Pop. 2,615. The v. is situated on the summit of an elevated and conspicuous ridge.

BLACK SEA, the ancient Pontus Euvinus, an immense inland sea, situated between Europe and Asia, and connected with the Mediterranean by the straits of Constantinople. It extends from 28° to 41° 30′ E long., or above 700 m.; and between the parallels of 41° and 47° N lat. Its breadth between the mouth of the Dnieper and the Bosphorus is nearly 400 m. Towards the centre it is contracted, by the projecting peninsula of the Crimea, terminating in Cape Aria, opposite to Cape Kerempe, to nearly 150 m. Its greatest breadth in its eastern part is 300 m. Its circuit approaches to 4,000 m. Its area is about 180,000 sq. m. On the N it washes the shores of Russian Tartary; on the E, it is bordered by Mingrelia, Circassia, and Georgia; on the S it laves the ever-smiling shores of Anatolia or Asia Minor; on the W, it has Romelia, Bulgaria, and Bessarabia. Origin of name.] The earliest Greek navigators who ventured into this sea, finding its shores occupied by fierce and inhospitable tribes, are reported to have at first given it the name of Pontus axenos, that is, 'the Inhospitable sea;' but when further intercourse had altered the spirit of these warlike tribes, and Grecian colonists had established themselves at different points of its shores, the epithet azzvos was changed to its contrary suzzvos, or 'Hospitable.' This is probably as fanciful as many other etymologies. The Black sea has received its present name from the darkness which often covers it, particularly during winter, in consequence of thick fogs and falling rains. This obscurity is sometimes so great that mariners are unable to see a cable's length from their vessels; and on these occasions the entrance of the Bosphorus is impracticable. Dr. Clarke affirms that there is no sea in which the navigation is more dangerous; that shallows hitherto unnoticed in any chart frequently occur when vessels are out of sight of land; and that dreadful storms come on so suddenly, and with such fury, that every mast is carried overboard almost as soon as the first symptom of a change of weather is observed. Admirals Priestman and Wilson, who commanded the Russian fleet, described the Black sea as exhibiting tempests more horrible than any thing they had ever encountered in the ocean. This account, however, is completely contrary to that given by Tournefort. "Whatever the ancients may have said," observes this able naturalist, "the Black sea has nothing black but the name; the winds do not blow there with more fury; and the storms are not more frequent than in other seas. We must pardon the exaggeration of the ancient poets, and particularly the chagrin of Ovid. The sand of the Black sea, indeed, is of the same colour with the sand of the White sea, and its waters are equally pure. If the coasts of this sea, in short, which are reckoned so dangerous, appear gloomy from a distance, it is owing to the woods which cover them, or the great distance which gives them the appearance of blackness. The sky here was so beautiful and so serene during the whole of our voyage, that we could not avoid giving the lie to Valerius Flaccus, the famous Latin poet, in describing the route of the Argonauts, who passed for the most celebrated sailors of antiquity, but who were only very little boys when compared with Vincent le Blanc, Tavernier, &c. This poet assures us, that the sky on the Black sea is always obscured with fogs." Mr. Thornton in his Survey of the Turkish Empire says: "It is a notion received among the Turks, that the Black sea is dangerous. To them indeed it is truly black; and it would even be so to British sailors in such vessels as the Turks use, and which are peculiar to that sea; they cannot lie-to, and are consequently obliged to run before the wind, and if they miss a port, go on shore. It is not more stormy than other seas." The rate of insurance on vessels navigating the Black sea shows that after all little account is now at least taken of the terrors of its navigation. Former state.] By comparing the accounts of this sea, as given by ancient authors, with the limits assigned to it by recent observations, it appears unquestionable that this sea has sustained a most singular diminution. From the topographical descriptions of the Euxine sea, as collected by Valerius Flaccus, and from the ancient accounts of the voyages of the Argonauts, it appears that the gulfs and bays of this sea were extremely deep; and that, in conjunction with the Palus Maeotidis, it extended far towards the N, and almost equalled the Mediterranean in magnitude. In the time of Homer, according to Strabo and Eustathius, the Euxine sea was regarded as the greatest of all inland seas, and received the name of Toytos, on account of its superior magnitude. Herodotus makes its length, from the Cyanean isles to the river Phasis, 11,100 stadia, or nearly 17 degrees and a half. Procopius reckons the distance from Chalcedon to the Phasis at 50 days' journey, for a good walker; which, at the rate of nine leagues a-day, will make the distance equal to 18 degrees,-a result which coincides remarkably with that of Herodotus. As the utmost extent of this sea, however, in the best modern charts, does not exceed 12 degrees and a half, we seem entitled to conclude, that it formerly covered the low grounds which stretch towards the base of the mountains of Caucasus.—The N and W coasts of this sea seem to have undergone very remarkable changes. The line of its greatest width has undoubtedly varied; and the immense volume of waters which is rolled from Asia and Europe into this capacious reservoir, seems to have completely changed the outline of its coast, and filled up the deep gulfs which indented its shores. The S coast, which consists chiefly of calcareous rocks, has suffered but few changes, excepting at the mouths of the rivers; and hence the geographical descriptions of this part of the Black sea, as given by ancient authors, are more easily reconciled with modern observations. From these indications of extensive changes, as well as from the testimony of ancient authors, it would appear, that the sea of Aral and the Caspian sea once formed an immense lake, joined by a strait to the Palus Mæotis and the Euxine sea; and that this huge collection of water was separated from the Mediterranean by a narrow isthmus formed by the Cyanean isles. An eruption of these volcanic islands is supposed to have formed an outlet for the exuberant waters of the Euxine, which rolled its torrent with irresistible impetuosity into the Propontis, and afterwards into the Mediterranean, deluging the low plains of Asia Minor, Thrace, Greece, Egypt, and Libya. The effects of this inundation are recorded in the monuments, the traditions, the poetry, the history, and the chronology of these ancient nations. The Samothracians, according to Diodorus, ascribed this deluge to the opening of the mouth of the Bosphorus. Their fishermen frequently dragged out, in their nets, the capitals of columns that had belonged to the submerged cities; and, in the time of Diodorus, they offered sacrifices upon altars erected on the line which formed the limits of the inundation. Ister, an ancient author quoted by Eustathius, mentions some great inundations, one of which opened the canal of the Hellespont that separated Europe from Asia; and Strabo and Xanthus bear testimony to the same event, which seems to have happened about 1529 years before Christ. We have also evidence in support of this remarkable fact, from the observations of modern naturalists and travellers. According to Tournefort, the Black sea appears to have been an immense lake, formed by the waters of innumerable rivers, and unconnected with the Mediterranean, and could only empty itself through the Thracian Bosphorus. The mountains which are interposed between it and the Caspian sea, prevent any opening towards the E. The waters of the sea of Azof fall into the Black sea, from N to S, and oppose any passage in that direction. The rivers of Asia impel the waters of the Black sea from S to N. The Danube impels them from the W. It is therefore at the isles of Cyaneæ alone, between the lighthouses of Europe and Asia, at the mouth of the Bosphorus, that the waters of the Black sea could hollow out the earth, and force a passage into the Propontis. Buffon likewise has adduced innumerable facts to prove that the Mediterranean sea, the Black sea, the Caspian sea, and the sea of Aral, have at one time been immense lakes; that the Caspian sea was formerly of great extent, and that the Mediterranean was once much smaller than it is at present; that the Aral, the Caspian, and the Black sea, formed only one lake before the opening of the Bosphorus; and that the Mediterranean, after this opening, was augmented in the same proportion as that lake had diminished. "Siberia, Asia, the Red sea, &c.," says Pallas, "present evident marks of this deluge, of which all the ancient people of Asia, the Chaldeans, the Persians, the Indians, the Thibetans, and the Chinese, have preserved the remembrance, and which they have fixed within a few years of the Mosaic deluge. Europe, and the low lands of Asia, have since suffered considerable changes by other inundations, sometimes arising from submarine eruptions, sometimes from the sudden overflow of the great inland seas, as the Mediterranean and the Euxine, which have left dry extensive plains covered with mud; and sometimes to irruptions of the sea, increased by enormous submarine eruptions." "The opinion of the indefatigable Tournefort and Buffon," the same naturalist elsewhere observes, "upon the ancient state of the Black sea, and its communication with the Caspian, has been more and more confirmed by the observations of travellers. The sea-monsters, the fish, and the marine shells, which the Caspian has in common with the Black sea, render this communication extremely probable; and the same facts prove also, that the lake of Aral was once joined to the Caspian. In the 3d and 7th volume of my Travels. I have traced the ancient extent of this sea over all the desert of Astracan and that of Jaik; by that appearance of coasts with which the elevated plains of Russia limit the desert,—by the state and the fossil productions of this ancient coast,-and by the salted mud mixed with calcined sea shells which cover all the surface of the desert." The Cyanean isles, at the mouth of the Bosphorus, have been examined by Olivier and Choiseul Gouffier, who discovered that these islands were volcanic. "At the mouth of the canal, on both sides of the Bosphorus," says Olivier, "we were struck with the marks of a volcano of several leagues in extent. Everywhere we observed the rocks more or less altered or decomposed; everywhere we found incontestable marks of the action of subterranean fires. We observed jaspers of different colours, cornelians, agates, and chalcedonies, among porphyries, more or less changed; a breccia, with little solidity, and almost decomposed, formed by fragments of trap, agglutinated by calcareous spar; a beautiful porphyry at the base of a rock of greenish trap, coloured by copper; and through an extent of half a league we saw a hard rock of trap, of a greenish blue, equally coloured with copper." In quoting this able traveller, on the volcanic nature of the banks of the Bosphorus, we ought at the same time to state that he entertains a different opinion from many other naturalists respecting the formation of that strait. He does not believe that the waters of the Black sea were once more elevated, and that they opened for themselves a passage by the Bosphorus; but he supposes that the Propontis, the Euxine, and the Mediterranean, always communicated with each other. He is of opinion, that the Caspian was of much greater extent; that it communicated with the sea of Azof, and that their waters had the same level; and he attributes to the currents of the Don, the Konban, and the Wolga, the filling up of the canal, and the consequent separation of the two seas. After this separation, he supposes that the Black sea has not changed its level, because it receives more water than it wastes by evaporation; that the Caspian sea has sunk above 60 ft., because it does not receive a quantity of water sufficient to supply what is lost by evaporation; and that it gradually diminished, till an equilibrium existed between the supply and the evaporation. M. Bergman, who travelled into the country of the Calmues in 1802 and 1803, has collected a vast number of facts, to prove that the Asiatic part of the steppes of the Calmucs was once covered with water; and that the Caspian and Black seas were formerly united. Dr. E. D. Clarke gives an interesting and doubtless accurate description of the Thracian Bosphorus, the Cyanean isles, and the adjoining strata, which may be here quoted to advantage: When Dr. C., in his voyage from Ineada to Constantinople, first discovered the light tower on the European side of the Bosphorus, it appeared situated at the base of an immense range of mountains. The whole coast, on both sides, opened with a degree of indescribable grandeur, resembling a stupendous wall, opposed to the great bed of waters, in which the mouth of the Bosphorus was like a small crack or fissure occasioned by an earthquake. "As we entered the straits," says Dr. C., "a miserable lantern, placed upon a tower on either side, presented to us all that was intended to serve as a guidance for seamen during the night. Never were lighthouses of more importance, or to which less attention has been paid. After passing the lighthouses, there appeared fortresses, the works of French engineers; and their situation, on rugged rocks, had a striking effect. Presently, such a succession of splendid objects was displayed, that, in all the remembrance of my former travels, I can recal nothing with which it may be compared. A rapid current, flowing at the rate of a league an hour, conveyed us from the Black sea. Then, while we were ruminating upon the sudden discharge of such accumulated waters by so narrow an aqueduct, and meditating the causes which first produced the wonderful channel through which they are conveyed, we found ourselves transported, as it were, in an instant, to a new world. Scarcely had we time to admire the extraordinary beauty of the villages, scattered up and down at the mouth of this canal, when the palaces and gardens of European and Asiatic Turks-the villas of foreign ambassadors, - mosques, minarets, mouldering towers, and ivymantled walls of ancient edifices, — made their appearance. Among these we beheld an endless variety of objects which seemed to realise tales of enchantment; fountains and cemeteries, hills, mountains, terraces, groves, quays, painted gondolas, and harbours, presented themselves to the eye, in such rapid succession, that, as one picture disappeared, it was succeeded by a second, more gratifying than the first. On the following day, we were determined to adventure an excursion as far as the islands anciently called Cyaneæ, or Symplegades, which lie off the mouth of the canal. The accurate Busbequius confessed, that, in the few hours he spent on the Black sea, he could discern no traces of their existence; we had, however, in the preceding evening, seen enough of them to entertain great curiosity concerning their nature and situation, even in the transitory view afforded by means of our telescopes. Strabo correctly describes their number and situation. 'The Cyaneæ,' says he, 'in the mouth of Pontus, are two little isles, one on the European, and the other on the Asiatic side of the strait, separated from each other by twenty stadia.' The more ancient accounts, which represented them as sometimes separated, and at other times joined together, were satisfactorily explained by Tournefort, who observed, that each of them consists of one craggy island, but that, when the sea is disturbed, the water covers the lower parts, so as to make the different points of either resemble insular rocks. They are, in fact, each of them joined to the main land by a kind of isthmus, and appear as islands when this is inundated, which always happens in stormy weather. But it is not clear that the isthmus, which connects either of them with the continent, was formerly visible. The disclosure has been probably owing to that gradual sinking of the level of the Black sea, before noticed. The same cause continuing to operate, may hereafter lead posterity to marvel what has become of the Cyaneæ; and this may also account for their multiplied appearance in ages anterior to the time of Strabo. The main object of our visit was not, however, the illustration of any ancient author in this particular part of their history, but to ascertain, if possible, by the geological phenomena of the coast, the nature of a revolution which opened the remarkable channel at the mouth of which those islands are situated. Some time before we reached the mouth of the canal, steering close along its European side, we observed the cliffs and hills, which are there destitute of verdure, presenting, even to their summits, a remarkable aggregate of enormous pebbles, that is to say, heterogeneous masses of stony substances, rounded by attrition in water, and imbedded in a hard natural cement, yet differing from the usual appearance of breecia rocks; for, upon a nearer examination, they appeared to have undergone, first, a violent action of fire, and secondly, that degree of friction, by long contact in water, to which their form was due. Breccia rocks do not commonly consist of substances so medified. The stratum formed by this singular aggregate, and the parts composing it, exhibited, by the circumstances of their position, striking proof of the power of an inundation; having dragged along with it all the component parts of the mixture, over all the heights above the present level of the Black sea, and deposited them in such a manner, as to leave no doubt concerning the torrent which passed toward the sea of Marmora. As in a field of corn long agitated by a particular wind, we see the whole crop incline towards one direction; so, at the mouth of the canal of Constantinople, all the strata of the mountains, and each individual mass composing them, lean from the N towards the S. On the point of the European lighthouse we found the sea, still tempestuous, beating against immense rocks of hard and compact lava; these had separated prismatically, and exhibited surfaces tinged by iron oxide wherever a division was effected. From this point we passed to the Cyanean isle, on the European side of the strait, and there landed. The structure of the rock of which the island consists, corresponds with the nature of the strata already described; but the substances composing it were perhaps never before associated in any mineral aggregate. They all appear to have been more or less modified by fire, and to have been cemented during the boiling of a volcano. In the same mass may be observed fragments of various coloured lava, trap, basalt, and marble. In the fissures are found agate, chalcedony, and quartz, but in friable and thin veins, not half-an-inch in thickness, and apparently deposited posterior to the settling of the stratum, of which the island consists. The agate appeared in a vein of considerable extent, occupying a deep fissure not more than an inch wide, and coated by a green substance, resembling some of the lavas of Etna, which have been decomposed by acidiferous vapours. Near the same vein appeared a substance resembling native mercury, but in such exceedingly minute particles, in a crumbling matrix, that it was impossible to preserve a specimen. The summit of this insular rock is the most favourable situation for surveying the mouth of the canal, which thus viewed has the appearance of a crater, whose broken sides opened towards the Black sea, and by a small aperture, towards the Bosphorus. The Asiatic side of the strait is distinguished by appearances similar to those already described, with this difference, that opposite to the island, a little to the east of the Anatolian lighthouse, a range of basaltic pillars may be discerned, standing upon a base inclined towards the sea; and, when examined with a telescope, exhibiting very regular prismatic forms. From the consideration of all the preceding observations, and comparing events recorded in history with the phenomena of nature, it is perhaps more than a conjectural position, that the bursting of the Thracian Bosphorus, the deluge mentioned by Diodorus Siculus, and the draining of the waters which once united the Black sea to the Caspian, and covered the great Oriental plain of Tartary, were all the consequence of earthquakes caused by subterranean fires, described as still burning at the time of the passage of the Argonauts, and whose effects are visible even at this hour." Coasts and Ports.] This sea is entered from the Mediterranean through the Dardanelles, the sea of Marmora, and the Bosphorus; and they who have held Constantinople, and its canal, have at all times exercised the greatest influence over its navigation and commerce. "Without her will," says Polybius, writing of Byzantium about 150 B. C., "no merchant can sail either to or from the Euxine." Off each point of the entrance from the Bosphorus lie the Cyanean rocks, or Symplegades. On quitting the Bosphorus, the W coast, from the European Pharos, in N lat. 41° 14' 10", E long. 29° 7' 20", runs in a curve as far as the gulf of Ainada or Iniada, a distance of 62 m.; and then turns NNW to the gulf of Burgas. This part of the coast is encircled by the ridges of Mount Hæmus and the Little Balkan which terminate in Cape Emineh, the Hæmi Extrema, in N lat. 42° 40½', E long. 27° 53' 42". The forest of Belgrade extends along this part of the coast, at some distance inland, for above 100 m. The chief towns which here present themselves are Mediah, Akteboli, and Burgas. To the gulf of Burgas succeeds that of Varna, whose S point, Cape Galata, is in N lat. 43° 10′, E long. 27° 58½′; and passing Cape Calagriah, in N lat. 43° 21' 10", E long. 28° 17' 15", Cape Chablersaghi, in N lat. 43° 32', and Cape Kustundji, in N lat. 44° 12', at 25 m. NE from the latter cape we reach Kara Kerman, whence the coast to Aktiar or Sebastopol, in the Crimea, is low, and lined with alluvial grounds and marshes. The Danube discharges itself, by several mouths, into the Black sea, between N lat. 44° 40' and 45° 30', and 17 leagues N 40° E from its N branch, is the S mouth of the Dniester, which divides Bessarabia from the Russian prov. of Cherson. All the coast between these two great rivers is so low that trees may be seen 5 or 6 m. off, before the shore itself is visible. The town of Akerman is 8 m. from the entrance of the Dniester; and 8 leagues NNE from the N mouth of that river lies Odessa, the great Russian port of the Black sea. The Dnieper, or Borysthenes, separates the Russian prov. of Cherson and Taurida; and the Bug, or Hypanis, flows into its liman, or long estuary, at a point bearing NNE, 100 leagues from the N entrance of the Bosphorus. On the r. bank of the Dnieper is Cherson, where the Russians build their line-of-battle ships; and at Nicolaief, on the Bug, are extensive marine arsenals, next in importance to those of Sebastopol. The peninsular projection of the Crimea lies between 32° 40' and 36° 30' E long. Its S point is Cape Saritche, in N lat. 44° 22'; and near to it is Sebastopol or Aktiar, the chief station of the Russian fleet in the B. sea. The S coast of the Crimea is lofty and precipitous, especially between Balaklava and Alushta. Towards Kaifa, the coast-range sinks gradually; and from Kaffa to Yenikale, it is prevailingly low, but shows here and there a few craggy points. The straits of Yenikalé, or of Kaffa, connecting the B. sea with the sea of Azov, I. show on the W side, or peninsula of Kertch, a range of hills rising to a height of 500 ft. at some points; but on the E side, or isle of Taman, the country consists of low strips of land interspersed with salt lakes. The isle of Taman is formed by branches of the Kuban, which drain the W side of the Caucasus range. A long sandy spit serves as a dyke to its waters on the E; and beyond it, in a SW direction, is the bay of Anapa, on the coast of the Abassian territory, 30 m. to the SE of which is Sujuk-Kaleh. The Russian boundary on the E coast of the B. sea is 3 leagues to the N of the river Tchorok, whence the coast trends SSW to the Rioni or Phasis, which has its mouth in N lat. 42° 8', E long. 41° 40'. Batum, in N lat. 41° 39', E long. 41° 38' 20", lies at the base of very high mountains; and hence to Rizeh, 26 m. E of Trebisond, the coast presents steep white cliffs. Trebisond, in N lat. 41° 1', E long. 39° 44', is next in rank to Odessa as a commercial port. Between it, and the mouth of the Kizil-Irmak, on the coast of Roum, are Vona, Ounieh, and Samsun. From the latter place to the vicinity of the E side of the Bosphorus, the coast of Anatolia is lined by high mountains terminating in lofty promontories, and forming a steep clean coast, with numerous little coves, into which small streams discharge themselves. The principal headland is Cape Kerempeh, the ancient Karambis, which forms the N point of Asia Minor, and is one of the highest points on the B. sea. From Cape Saritche, the S point of the Crimea, it bears S 7° W 144 geog. m. It is the imaginary line between these two remarkable capes which divides the B. sea into two parts; and the configuration produced by the advance of the land here, and its curve falling back on each side, in the estimation of the ancients, gave to the whole sea the outline of a Scythian bow. The principal rivers in this last section of the coast are the Kizil-Irmak or Halys, the Sakaria or Sangarius, the Falios or Bileaus, and the Bartin or Parthenius. Winds and Currents.] N winds prevail in the B. sea towards the middle of summer; and NE winds from June to August; and when these blow long, the current impelled through the Bosphorus is often so strong as to detain vessels for months at the other end of the straits. Towards the close of summer, S winds prevail; and these are also frequent, from SSE and SW, in January, February, and March. Gales sometimes occur, but rarely last above twelve hours, and are seldom of extreme violence. The waters of this sea generally set in the direction of the prevailing wind, and are of course determined, in different localities, by that of the large rivers which discharge themselves into this sea; but the prevailing tendency of the whole mass of waters is towards the Bosphorus, from which one portion deflected runs along the coast of Asia towards the E, and then turns N along the Mingrelian and Circassian coasts. The strength of the current which sets in through the Bosphorus from this sea will be better understood when the extent of water-drainage which the B. sea receives is considered. It is probable that the various rivers which flow into it drain a surface of country in Europe and Asia little short of 1,000,000 sq. m. And this circumstance likewise accounts for the comparative freshness of its water. Climate.] The climate of the B. sea is in general cold. All the rivers and ports on its N coasts are frozen more or less during winter; and snow rests on many points of the surrounding mountains all the year. The Bosphorus, however, was never known to be frozen over. Ovid, during his residence on the Black sea, had observed the "ingentem glacie consistere pontum;" and Dr. Clarke was informed by Captain Bergamini that he was once detained five months in the mouth of the Danube by the freezing of Fish.] The tunny, sturgeon, porpoise, mackarel, sole, turbot, the sea. whiting, and roach, are found in this sea. Of these, the tunny are sometimes very abundant. Caviar is made on the coasts, as well as fish-glue, fish-oil, and botargo, a preparation from the sea-mullet. Salted and smoked mackarel form an important article in the commerce of the Crimea. This sea is infested by a species of sea-worm, which gnaws the planks of vessels, and in the space of two years completely destroys the sides of the ships. These animals are four or five inches long; their head resembles an arrow, and their body consists of a whitish mucilage. The only way of destroying them has been, until lately, to lay up the vessels for two years to careen them, and to cover the sides with burning pitch and juniper wood. The vessels which navigate the Black sea are now coppered, which is the only effectual remedy against the attack of these worms. Commerce.] We shall conclude this article by giving some account of the commerce of the Black sea, for which we are principally indebted to M. Reuilly, who travelled along its shores in 1803. "The storms frequent in the Black sea," observes that learned traveller, "and the savage state of the people inhabiting its coasts, prevented the Greeks for a long time from visiting its shores. The expedition of the Argonauts is the first trace of navigation and commerce in this sea, which antiquity has transmitted to us. This trade took place principally in the Oriental parts; but notwithstanding the establishment of several colonies upon its coasts, it was inconsiderable during the first ages of Greece, and under the empire of the Romans. It did not begin to flourish until the time of the Crusades, when the Latins possessed themselves of Constantinople. At that time the Genoese and the Venetians carried on this trade with such considerable advantage that the conquest of Egypt by the Arabs having entirely ruined the ancient commerce of Alexandria, the merchandise of India opened itself a new way to the European markets. Merchants went thither sometimes by the Indies, and the Russian sea, or by caravans across Georgia and Mingrelia; sometimes by going up the Persian gulf, the Tigris, or the Euphrates; or by way of Armenia, to Trebizond, whither the Genoese and the Venetians went to meet them, for the purpose of supplying Europe with their commodities. Commercial jealousy, the inevitable consequence of the great advantages of this trade, was the occasion of bloody wars between the Venetians and the Genoese, which terminated in favour of the latter, who, by contributing to the overthrow of the dominion of the Latins at Constantinople, and profiting dexterously by the favour or the weakness of the Greek emperor, obtained such advantages that they had no longer any rivals. To secure to themselves exclusively this commerce, they fortified their settlement at Pera, established colonies on the coasts, principally in the Crimea, and put their factories in a state of defence. Caffa was the principal city of their commerce with the East, and the port at which was deposited all the merchandise transported to the Black sea. The wares of India, Persia, and Arabia, came to Astracan, were sent up the Volga, carried afterwards by land as far as the Don, conveyed by that river to Azof, and thence embarked for Caffa. The Genoese enjoyed these advantages until the taking of Constantinople by Mahomet, of which the immediate consequence was their expulsion from the Crimea. With the annihilation of the power of the Genoese, ended the commerce of the Black sea." It is to be observed that, at this epoch, the re-establishment of the ancient route by Alexandria, which took place under Tala-Eddin, had already turned that source of riches from this line. The progress of navigation, the discovery of the route to India, and of America, gave a new spring to the mercantile spirit of Europe, lessening, in some degree, the regret which the loss of this traffic had occasioned; but it was not until the beginning of the 18th cent, that Peter I., desirous to create commerce, then unknown in his vast empire, saw the immense advantages to be derived from the possession of some ports in the Black sea. He succeeded in the acquisition of Azof; but the misfortunes which he met with afterwards, and the peace of Pruth, were the occasion of his surrendering these conquests, and the advantages which might have been derived from them. Catherine II., following the steps of this great legislator of Russia, had the glory of accomplishing the design his genius had conceived. After two long wars, the Turks found themselves compelled to surrender to Russia a part of Lesser Tartary, and ultimately the Crimea itself; to allow the Russians to establish a navy in the Black sea, and to permit their flag the free passage of the Dardanelles. The Turks excluded all foreign vessels from its waters at the peace of Pruth in 1739—and subsequently, in that of Belgrade, Russia expressly renounced the navigation of this sea; but the treaty of Kainardji, in 1774, reopened its navigation to the Russians, and they have since made themselves masters of the whole of the northern coast. The Porte indeed claims to have the command and control of the Bosphorus; but by article 7th of the treaty of Adrianople, "the passage of the canal of Constantinople and of the Dardanelles is declared free to all merchant-vessels of the powers at peace with the Porte, whether they are proceeding to Russian ports in the Black sea, or returning from them loaded or unloaded, on the same conditions as vessels under the Russian flag; and the Porte solemnly pledges herself never to raise any obstacle to this right." Turkey promises never to stop the vessels, either laden or not laden, whether belonging to Russia or any other country—not at war with the Sultan—passing through the canal of Constantinople, and the Dardanelles, from the Black sea to the Mediterranean, or from the Mediterranean to the Russian ports of the Black sea; and in case of any contravention not followed by immediate satisfaction at the demand of the Russian ambassador, the Porte recognises the right of Russia to consider such an act an act of hostility, and to make reprisals. The sultan also pledges himself to exclude all vessels of war. This treaty seems artfully contrived to place the domination of the Black sea entirely under Russian influence: for in the event of a war between England and Russia, while the latter power has an armed squadron in the Black sea, England could not protect her merchant-vessels in that sea. The treaty of Kainardji, concluded between Russia and Turkey towards the close of the last cent., opened to Europeans the navigation of the Black sea, from which they had been excluded during upwards of 300 years. On the 21st of July, 1774, the prohibition to enter the Bosphorus was raised, and vessels were at last permitted to penetrate into that sea which no European had navigated since the expulsion of the Genoese, who remained masters until the 15th cent. of the principal harbours of the Crimea. The Empress Catherine II. cast her eyes with complacency on a country which, after six consecutive campaigns, had been at last united to her extensive dominions; and she resolved to found on its shores a number of commercial and military establishments. One of the largest rivers of Russia, the Dnieper, empties itself into the Black sea; her attention was directed to it, and, in 1778, General Hannibal, by her orders, laid the foundation of the town of Kherson. In 1783, a Frenchman established there the first commercial house, and undertook to supply the arsenal of Toulon with the flax and timber which descended the Dnieper. The partition of Poland gave a new direction to the commercial ideas of Catherine II. She perceived the necessity of possessing more to the westward, on the coast of the Black sea, a harbour for the exportation of the agricultural productions of the palatinates of Bruslaw, Podolia, Wolhynia, and other provinces recently incorporated with the Russian empire. She accordingly determined on the foundation of Odessa, which took place in 1796. The fault committed at Kherson,—the development of which was arrested by the exigencies of the Russian system of customs,was avoided at Odessa by the Emperor Alexander: in 1803 that prince reduced by one fourth the import and export duties established by the general tariff in all the harbours of the Black sea. In 1804 he decreed the opening at Odessa of an entrepôt for goods imported by sea, the introduction of which was permitted in Russia, and where they were permitted to remain during 18 months without custom duties or storage, until they should be sold, and on the point of being forwarded to the interior of the empire. By an ukase of the same year (March 5), it was provided that all foreign goods the importation of which was permitted at Odessa, and those transported thither from other towns of Russia, should be allowed to pass in transitu, free of all charge, for Moldavia and Wallachia, through the custom-offices of Mohilew and Doubassaa; for Austria, through that of Radzilow; for Russia, through that of Kezinsky; and that foreign goods sent to Odessa by those four custom-offices should enjoy a free transit by sea. Agriculture, throughout Europe, had suffered during the war; and Odessa was the granary of the countries where scarcity prevailed. But the spirit of wisdom did not long preside over the councils of Russia. She imprudently adopted the prohibitive system, the consequences of which are ever fatal; dan laboured with as much ardour to destroy the commercial prosperity of Southern Russia as she had displayed to create it. In 1822, when it was least expected, an ukase suppressed the freedom of the harbour of Odessa. Goods, on importation, were subjected to pay one-fifth of the duty required in other Russian harbours; and on their removal to the interior, the four other fifths. The limits of the free harbour were also considerably curtailed; two lines of customs were formed, one round the harbour, and the other round the city. Those lines still exist to the present day. Many other deplorable blows were levelled at the commerce of Odessa. The freedom of the Cancasian provinces was suddenly withdrawn; and the merchants of Western Asia, who were beginning to visit Odessa, on their way to the great fairs of Germany, proceeded thither by another route. England took advantage of the faults of Russia, opened commercial relations with Trebizond and her merchants, formed there an immense entrepôt, and thence exported her manufactures to all the provinces of Asia. The trade of Trebizond now exceeds £2,000,000; and it communicates with Constantinople by two regular lines of steam-packets. Southern Russia is now threatened, in the Black sea, by a competition, becoming daily more formidable. Thanks to the freedom and privileges they have derived from the treaty of Adrianople, the Danubian principalities have within the last ten years made immense progress. Galatz and Ibrail now ship a considerable quantity of grain to foreign countries; and, notwithstanding the difficulty of ascending the Danube, the captains prefer repairing to those harbours, on account of the facilities afforded by their administration, and the commercial resources offered to importation. The trade of Southern Russia is consequently very far from prospering. In 1839, the most productive year, the customs-duties produced in all only 8,215,426 rubles. The different harbours, situate along an extent of coast of upwards of 400 leagues, and the three land customoffices, exported, on an average, only from 45,000,000 to 55,000,000 francs, and imported about one-third of that sum; whilst the single harbour of Trebizond sends annually to the different adjoining countries upwards of 50,000,000 francs worth of English manufactures. [Semaphore de Marseilles.] According to Hagemeister's official report, the total value of imports at the port of Odessa, in 1840, was £962,018; and in 1842, £985,510. The value of exports for 1840 was £1,986,526; and in 1842, £1,881,505. The chief article of export was wheat, of which, in 1840, 568,055 quarters were exported; and in 1842, 621,664 quarters, of which 200,592 quarters were for England. The value of imports at Odessa by British ships, in 1842, was £184,370, of which £60,000 was in woollen, cotton, and silk goods, and £44,200 in refined sugar. Metals and furs formerly ranked next to grain in the exports of Odessa, but these articles have been abandoned to the Baltic ports, where they can be supplied cheaper. "The commerce of the Black sea, however," continues Hagemeister, "has been well-compensated for their loss by numberless articles of trade drawn from the provinces of the S and W of Russia, such as wool, wax, hempen cordage, flax, linseed, hempseed, and various other seeds from which oil is obtained. These articles are rapidly giving a new feature to our commerce. By the customhouse statements, their value as articles of exportation, in 1815, with the single exception of cordage, did not reach, at Odessa, 250,000 rubles: in 1832 the value of these exports exceeded 5,000,000 rubles. In 1833 and 1834 they became the staple articles of our commerce; and we may, I think, calculate on a period not very remote, when grain shall be no longer considered our principal export article. Indeed, for some time past, the supply of the articles above-mentioned has fallen considerably short of the demand. The exportation of linseed from the Black sea, which, in 1830, was not more than 6,000 chetwerts, rose in 1833, to 70,000, more than 65,000 of which were shipped entirely from Odessa. The timber-trade, from which much was expected in 1802, has not proved so lucrative as was anticipated; but wool both of coarse and fine quality, is daily becoming a more important export, as might have been expected from the vast ranges of pasturage in Southern Russia. There is, however, no great prospect of this article being supplied at such a rate as would render it a lucrative article of import to British manufacturers, especially as the Merino breed has not been yet extensively introduced into these regions. In the course of ten years, reckoning from 1824, the quantity of wool shipped to foreign countries, from all the Russian ports of the Black sea, and the sea of Azof, was 570,000 poods, a little less than one-third part of which was exported in 1833. Tallow is an article of greater importance. The English were the first to perceive the immense advantages that could be drawn from the tallow of Southern Russia, and to give an impulse to this trade. In 1814, the value of tallow exported from Odessa, was only 84,554 rubles; in 1834 it amounted to more than 9,000,000 rubles, which is more than 100 times the original value. But Odessa, independent of its foreign trade, contains within itself some important commercial advantages. In the coasting trade of the Black sea, two steam-vessels, each of 70 horse-power, are sent out from this port; and two others, of betwixt 80 to 100 horse-power, ply betwixt Odessa and Constantinople. The fisheries of the Black sea have been equally celebrated in ancient and modern times, but this branch of industry is said to be in a very backward state, the only article of export derived from it is caviar. With regard to stuffs of European manufacture, Odessa imports the largest quantity, owing to the great consumption of these articles in the bosom of the free port. The value of woollen imported occasionally exceeds 1,000,000; that of cotton-goods constantly exceeds 500,000, and that of silk goods 300,000, or 400,000 rubles yearly. But the quantity imported of this latter article remains almost stationary, whereas that of the other stuffs is rapidly increasing.—The transit trade between Odessa and the Transcaucasian provinces, by the route of Redout- Kalé, in 1823 rose suddenly into importance, when the Armenian merchants at Tiflis, not only began to make their purchases in Odessa, but after they passed to the fair of Leipsic, sent home their goods via Odessa and Redout-Kalé. But the importunities of the Russian manufacturers induced the Emperor to extend the European tariff beyond the Caucasus, from the mouth of the Kuban to that of the Terek; and, in consequence, the trade of Redout-Kalé, which, in 1828, exceeded 2,000,000 of rubles, fell below 20,000 in 1832, and is now even further diminished. "It is," continues Hagemeister, "for the benefit of Trebisond, and of the English—who have formed considerable establishments there —that Russia has closed the port of Redout-Kalé. The only duties paid at Trebisond are 2 per cent, to the warehouse-keepers, and per cent. for the right of sale. On the route from Trebisond to Tauris there is no duty demanded in the Turkish dominions; but having once arrived in Persia, foreigners pay 5 per cent., and the Persians 2 per cent., exclusive of 228 copecks in silver for every load. By this route England and Germany supply Persia and Anatolia with woollen cloths, ladies' cloths, printed calicoes, cotton thread, paper, sugar, coffee, glass, porcelain, iron, tin, and steel articles."
