BLACKSIDE-END, a mountain on the NE boundary of the p. of Sorn in Ayrshire. Alt. 1,500 ft.

BLACKSOD, a promontory and bay on the coast of co. Mayo, 9½ m. NE by N of Achill-head. The mouth of the bay is 4½ m. wide; and it penetrates the main northward 10 m. Its breadth varies from 5 m. to 300 or 400 ft.

BLACKSTAIRS, a range of mountains, whose watershed forms, over a considerable distance, the boundary-line between co. Carlow and that of Wexford. The range is about 13 m. long, and extends SSW. It commences in Mount Leinster, 3\frac{1}{2} m. SW by S of Newtownbarry, and terminates 5 m. NNE of New Ross. The summits exhibit softly-rounded outlines; yet they have a varied sky-line, and their green sides abound in picturesque and romantic ravines and deep dells. Scallagh pass, near the middle of the range, is traversed by the road from Enniscorthy to Killedmon. The principal summits and their respective alt., named in a series from N to S, are Mount Leinster, 2,610 ft., -Knockroe, 1,746 ft., - Blackstairs proper, 2,406 ft., —White Mountain, 1,679 ft., -and Carriglead, 1,053 ft.
BLACKSTOCKS, a village in Chester district, in the state of S Carolina, U.S., 46 m. N by W of Columbia.

BLACKSTONE, or PAWTUCKET, a river in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., an affluent of Providence river in Rhode island. The B. canal from the B.'s scenery is so rich that it might fill and em- BLA Worcester to Providence, 45 m. in length, follows the course of this river.
BLACKSTONE-EDGE, a high ridge of hills, 6 m. ENE of Rochdale, separating Lancashire and Yorkshire in that quarter.
BLACKSTONES, a stream in co. Kerry, flowing 7 m. N into Lough Carragh.

BLACKSVILLE, a village in Monongalia co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., at the junction of Robert's Run and Drunkard's creek, 50 m. above the entrance of the latter into the Monongahela. The creek is navigable to this place.
BLACK SWAMPS, a village in Sandusky co., in the state of Ohio, 113 m. N of Columbus.
BLACKTHORN, a hamlet in the p. of Ambroseden, in Oxfordshire, 3 m. SE by E of Bicester. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 380.
BLACKTOFT, a parish and township in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SE by E of Howden. Area 2,730 acres. Pop. 505, of whom 374 were in the township.
BLACK TORRINGTON. See TORRINGTON (BLACK).

BLACKWALL, an important district in the p. of Poplar All-Saints, in Middlesex, 4 m. E by S of St. Paul's, connected with London by the B. railway which forms the grand connecting link between the metropolis and the river traffic. This railway runs from Fenchurch street to the Brunswick wharf at Blackwall. Its total length is 3 m. 843 y.—The East India docks are situated at B. They consist of an entrance-basin of 23 acres; an export basin of about 9 acres; and an import basin of 18 acres. In front of the docks is a cast-iron wharf 750 ft. in length.

BLACK WARRIOR, a river of Alabama, U. S., formed by Locust fork and Mulberry river. which unite above Tuscaloosa, and, after a course of 80 m., enter the Tombigbee. The whole course of the river is about 160 m., and it is navigable for steamboats to Tuscaloosa, and for boats much higher up.

BLACK WATER, a river of Dorsetshire, rising near Cranbrook, and flowing into the Stour.—Also a river of Essex, which rises near Wimbish, and flows in a SE and S course 30 m. to near Witham, where it is joined by Pod's brook; it then flows S 4 m. to its junction with the Chelmer; after which it flows E, 12 m. to the bay of the same name. Its estuary is from 1 to 2 m. wide at high water; and contains several small islands.

BLACKWATER, a river of Munster, probably the second of the Irish rivers in bulk, and certainly one of its richest in beauty. It rises between the mountains of Knocknagossy and Knockanadrine, only 15 m. E of the head of Tralee bay; runs southward about 12 m.; then runs E about 37 m. through Cork, and 12 m. to Cappoquin; and finally runs 8 m. S through Waterford, to the head of its estuary usually called Youghal harbour. These measurements are made nearly in straight lines, and stated in Irish reckoning; if augmented by following the river's sinuosities, and reduced to statute reckoning, they would fall little short of the measurements of the Welsh and English Severn. The Blackwater's chief tributaries are the Cledogh, on its r. bank; and the Allua, the Awbey, the Funcheon, and the Araglin, on its l., in co. Cork; and the Bride, on its r. bank, and the Finisk and Lickey, on its l., in co. Waterford. The principal towns immediately on its banks are Mallow, Fermoy, Lismore, Cappoquin, and Youghal. The river is navigable for barges to the Bride's mouth, about 12 m. from the bar; for lighters, to Lismore canal, 20 m. from the bar; and for flatbottomed boats and yawls, to Mallow, or higher. It is tidal for 20 m. from its mouth.—The subject of bellish a whole volume. "We have had descents of the Danube," says Mr. Inglis, "and descents of the Rhine and of the Rhone, and of many other rivers, but we have not in print, so far as I know, any descent of the B.; and yet with all these descents of foreign rivers in my recollection, I think the descent of the B. not surpassed by any of them."-Also a river of Ulster, the largest in the vast basin of Lough Neagh, except the Upper Bann. It rises 21 m. N of Five-mile-town, and falls into the SW corner of Lough Neagh, after a NE course of 40 m. Its tributaries are numerous; but, excepting the Callen, which joins it from the SE, they are individually inconsiderable. The principal towns and villages on its banks are Clogher, Augher, Aughnacloy, Caledon, Benburb, and Moy in Tyrone, and Blackwatertown and Charlemont in Armagh.—Also a river of co. Cavan, which rises in two head-streams, in Clonkee. These streams flow in a direction W of S, to the head of Lough Ramor; and the more easterly of the two, in consequence of its entering the lake at Virginia, is called Virginia water. The efflux of superfluent waters from the lake alone bears the name of the B., in popular nomenclature; it runs 15 m. ESE to the Boyne at Navan. Its chief tributary, the Moynalty, falls upon its l. side, 3 m. below Kells.— Also a highly picturesque mountain-rivulet of co. Kerry, which rises among the Dunkerrin mountains, but is popularly regarded as issuing from Lough Brin; and flows about 8 m. S to the Kenmare estuary. The B. and its chief affluent, the Awrua, drain an extent of bog amounting to 4,690 acres.—Also a sluggish and boggy rivulet of King's co., which rises 11 m. NE of Ferbane, and flows 7 m. W to the Shannon. It traverses, from its source to very nearly its embouchure, an irksome expanse of bog; and, jointly with its affluents, probably drains more morass than any other equal extent of stream in Ireland. The chief division of the bogs within its basin takes name from it, comprehends an area of 12,105 acres, and extends, with various breadth, 8 m. E from Shannon-bridge to the vicinity of Balnahown. Its surface varies in height from 10 to 58 ft. above the level of the Shannon. Its depth averages about 30 ft., and in some places is 40 or 44 ft.—Also a rivulet rising in co. Kildare, and running 13 m. NNW to the Boyne, in the vicinity of Castle-Rickard.
BLACKWATER, a village, partly in the p. of Ballyvalden, and partly in that of Killila, co. Wexford, a short distance SW of Oulart. Pop. 380.

BLACKWATER, a river in Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., which enters the Contoocock.—Also a river in Virginia, which enters the Nottaway after a course of 70 m.—Also a village in Pettis co., Missouri. Pop. 197.
BLACKWATER LAKE, a river of N America, 60 m. S of Great Bear lake, in N lat. 64° 10′, discharging itself into the Mackenzie river on the r. bank.
BLACKWATERTOWN, a village in the p. of Confeale, in co. Armagh, on the r. bank of the Blackwater, 24 m. S by W of Moy, near the junction of the Ulster canal with the Blackwater. Pop. 369.

BLACKWELL, a township in the p. of Bakewell, in Derbyshire, 3\frac{1}{4} m. SW of Tidswell. Pop. 68.— Also a parish in Derbyshire, 3 m. NE of Alfreton. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 477.—Also a township in the p. of Darlington, in Durham, 1 m. SW by S of Darlington, on the E bank of the Tees. Area 1,670 acres. Pop. 299.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Tredington, in Worcestershire, 21 m. NNW of Shipstoneupon-Stour. Pop. 204.—Also two townships in the p. of St. Cuthbert, in Cumberland, distinguished as and Low B. The former is 21 m. S of Carlisle. Pop. 315. The latter is 2 m. S of Carlisle. Pop. 181. BLA

BLACKWOOD, a river of Western Australia, in Sussex co., flowing into Hardy's inlet and Port Augusta. Its banks are steep, and clothed with enormous trees. It has been traced 40 or 50 m. from its junction with the Chapman, and little alteration in its size or appearance was perceptible throughout this distance.
BLACKWOOD BAY, a bay in Australia, in Torres strait, in S lat. 10° 383', E long. of Port Essington, 10° 28'.
BLACKWOOD AND CROWBOROUGH, a township in Horton p., in Staffordshire, 4 m. W of Leek. Pop. 526.
BLACON-CUM-CRABHALL, a township in the p. of Holy Trinity, Cheshire, 2 m. WSW of Chester. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 61.

BLADEN, a town of Prussian Silesia, in the gov. of Oppeln, circle of Leobschütz. Pop. 1,200.—Also a county in the S part of North Carolina, U.S., watered by Cape Fear river. Area 1,200 sq. m. Pop. 8,022. Cap. Elizabeth, on Cape Fear river, 40 m. above Wilmington.

BLADENOCH, a river of Wigtonshire, which rises in Loch Machbeary, on the borders of Carrick, and after a winding but prevailingly SSE course of 24 m., flows into Wigton bay, a little to the SW of the town of Wigton.
BLADENSBURG, a village in Prince George co., in the state of Maryland, U. S., 6 m. NE of Washington. Pop. 400.-Also a v. in Knox co., in Ohio, 61 m. NE of Columbus.
BLADIAU, a town of Prussia, 22 m. SW of Konigsberg, near the E shore of the Frische-haff.
BLADOLSHEIM. See BLOTZHEIM.

BLADON, a parish in Oxfordshire, 2 m. S of Woodstock. Area 1,350 acres. Pop., with Hensington, 687. BLÆFELL [i. e. 'Blue mountain'], the SE division of an extensive chain of ice-mountains, in Iceland, called the Langi Yökul. It lies about 30 m. N of Hecla; and its highest summit appears to be a huge extinct volcano.—Henderson.
BLAEN-AERON, a township in the p. of Caronys-Clawdd, in Cardiganshire, 4 m. NE of Tregaron. Pop. 287.
BLAEN-CARON, a township in the p. of Caronys-Clawdd, in Cardiganshire. Pop. 111.
BLAENEGEL, a hamlet in Llanguick p., in Glamorganshire. Pop. 182.
BLAEN-PORTH, a parish in Cardiganshire, 6 m. E by N of Cardigan. Pop. 752.
BLAENGURACH, a chapelry in Glamorganshire, in Glyn-Corrwg p., 10 m. NE of Neath. Pop. 498.
BLAENHONDDAN, a hamlet in Cadoxton p., in Glamorganshire, 3 m. NNW of Neath. Pop. 1,156.
BLAENPENAL, a chapelry in Llan-ddewi-brefi, p., in Cardiganshire, 11 m. N by E of Lampeter. Pop. 503.
BLAENSAWTHE, a hamlet in Llanddausaint p., in Carmarthenshire. Pop. 214.
BLAENYNIS (LLAN), a hamlet in Carmarthenshire, in the p. of Llangathen. Pop. with Cwmysigifarowg, 351.

BLAGDON, a township in the p. of Stannington, in Northumberland, 7 m. S of Morpeth. Pop. 95. —Also a tything in the p. of Cranborne, Dorsetshire. Pop. 36.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 6 m. NE of Axbridge. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 1,178.
BLAGNAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. and 2 m. NNW of Toulouse, on the l. bank of the Garonne. Pop. 1,476.
BLAGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Carignan. Pop. 304.
BLAGOVESTE, a town of Servia, in the pash. of Semendria, NW of Kulielava. ^
BLAGOVIESTCHENSK, a town of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, on the r. side of the Tura, W of Turinsk.
BLAGRAVE, a tything in Lambourn p., in Berkshire. Pop. 204.
BLAID. See Beled.

BLAIN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Savenay.—The cant., comprising 4 com., had a pop. of 12,123 in 1831.—The com. is 20 m. NNW of Nantes, on the Isac. Pop. 4,899. There are zinc works here; and the remains of an ancient castle, once one of the strongest fortresses in Bretagne, and vainly besieged in 1589.
BLAINEY, a parcel in Llanfihangel-Cwmdu p., in Breconshire. Pop. 140.
BLAINVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Saint-Malo-de-la-Lande, near the coast, 6 m. W of Coutances. Pop. 1,770.
BLAINVILLE SUR L'EAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Bayon, on the Meurthe, 5 m. SW of Luneville. Pop. 820.

BLAIR-ATHOLE, an extensive parish in the district of Athole, in Perthshire, comprising the united parishes of Blair-Athole and Strowan, which extend upwards of 30 m. in length; and allowing for the ascents and descents of the hills, 18 m. in breadth. Their superficial area has been estimated at 312 sq. m. They are bounded on the N by Inverness-shire; on the NE by Aberdeenshire; on the E by Kirkmichael and Moulin parishes; on the S by Dull parish; and on the W by Rannoch. An extensive well-cultivated strath or valley lies along the Garry; and Strath-Tummel runs along the loch of that name, which is about 2 m. long. Between these two straths is a stretch of moorland about 4 m. in breadth. The rest of the parish consists of small straths along the rivulets which descend from the mountains, naked rocks, and extensive moor-clad hills. On the summits of the higher mountains, we find little else than gravel and stones covered with moss. Farther down occur heath, Uva ursi, and the crawberry; in boggy places, the cloudberry; and on drier ground, the whortleberry, with coarse grass. Still lower down, amidst heath and peat-bog, small valleys with pretty good pasture appear, and here and there a green spot with huts to which the herds retire with the cattle in the summer season. The most remarkable mountain is Beinndeirg, or Bendearg, i. e., 'the Red mountain,' so called from a vein of red granite which intersects it. It rises 3,550 ft. above the level of the sea; but is exceeded by Bengloe, or Benygloe, the highest pinnacle of which, Carn-nan-gour, or Carn-nan-Gabhar, i. e. 'the Mountain of goats,' rises 3,690 ft. above sealevel. There are several lochs, and two considerable rivers, the Tummel and the Garry, in this p. The principal streams are the Erochty or Erickhie, the Bruar, and the Tilt, all tributaries of the Garry. The prevailing kinds of timber are birch, alder, ash, oak, poplar, and hazel. Pop. in 1801, 2,848; in 1831, 2,384; in 1841, 2,231. Houses in 1831, 520; in 1841, 486.—The village of Blair-Athole is situated on the road from Edinburgh to Fort Augustus, 28 m. from Perth, and 76 m. from Edinburgh. Within a furlong of it is the village of Bridge-of-Tilt. Both these villages are of modern erection, and remarkably pleasing appearance.—In the vicinity of the v. of Blair-Athole is Blair castle, an ancient fortalice, and re-edified mansion, which was selected to be the summer residence of Queen Victoria in 1844.

BLAIR-GOWRIE, a large parish in Perthshire, skirted by the river Isla on the N, and by the Ericht on the E. Pop. in 1801, 1,914; in 1841, 3,471. A considerable amount of linen is woven in this p. for BLA the Dundee and London manufacturers.-The v. of B. is pleasantly situated on the N side of Strathmore, near the Ericht. Pop. in 1841, 1,242.
BLAIR-LOGIE, a pleasant village in the p. of Logie, in Stirlingshire, at the entrance of Glen-Devon.
BLAIR'S FERRY, a village in Roane co., in Tennessee, U. S., 165 m. E of Nashville.
BLAIRSTOWN, a village in Warren co., in New Jersey, 74 m. N by W of Trenton.

BLAIRSVILLE, a township in Indiana co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 171 m. W by N of Harrisburg, on the Kiskiminitas river, and the line of the Pennsylvania and Ohio canal. Pop. 990.-Also a village in York district, in S Carolina, 88 m. N of Columbia. —Also a village in Union co., in the state of Georgia, 118 m. NNW of Milledgeville.
BLAISDON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. N of Newnham. Area 760 acres. Pop. 264.

BLAISE, a river of France, which rises near Gillancourt, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, and flowing N, in a direction nearly parallel to the Marne, passes Doulevant-le-Chateau, Vassy, and Eclaron; it then turns W, and flows into the Marne near Arigny, after a course of 55 m.

BLAISE (SAINT), or SAINT BLASIE, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 3 m. NE of Neufchatel, on the l. bank of the lake of Neufchatel. The Moravian brethren have a community in the neighbourhood of this place at Montmirail.

BLAISE HILL, a hill which rises on the SW of the village of Henbury, 5 m. NNW of Bristol, on the summit of which are the remains of a British fort, one of a chain of fortresses and beacons which anciently extended through the vale of the Severn.
BLAISOIS, a district of France, in the Orléanais, now comprised in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher. Its cap. is Blois. Area 185,971 hectares.
BLAISON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Les Ponts-de-Cé, on the l. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,142.

BLAIZE (CAPE St.), a promontory on the coast of S Africa, in S lat. 34° 18', E long. 22° 18', 6 m. W of the mouth of the Gouritz. It forms the SW point of Mossel bay, and the extremity of a ridge of hills composed of crystalline quartz intermixed with silicious sandstone.
BLAIZE. See BLAS.
BLAKELEY, a township in Luzerne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 150 m. NE of Harrisburg, on Lackawannock river. Pop. 570.

BLAKELY, a village in Early co., in the state of Georgia, U. S., 203 m. SW of Milledgeville.—Also a port-of-entry, and the cap. of Baldwin co., in the state of Alabama, on the E side of the Tonsaw river, 10 m. ENE of Mobile. The harbour, which is formed in the E outlet of Mobile river, admits vessels drawing 11 ft. water.
BLAKEMERE, a parish in Herefordshire, 101 m. W of Hereford. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 183.

BLAKENEY, a parish and seaport in Norfolkshire, 8 m. E of Wells. Area of p. 1,630 acres. Pop. 1,021. Between 50 and 60 vessels, averaging 60 tons burden, and employed in the coasting-trade and oyster-fisheries, belong to this port.
BLAKENHALL, a township in Wybunbury p., in Cheshire, 6 m. SE of Nantwich. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 257.
BLAKENHAM (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Suffolk; the former 3½ m., the latter 4 m. SE of Needham. Area of Great B., 1,850 acres. Pop. 180. Area of Little B., 250 acres. Pop. 119.
BLAKESLEY, a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. WNW of Towcester. Area 2,840 acres. Pop., with Woodend hamlet, 830.
BLAKIER, a village of Norway, in the prov. of Christiania, and bailiwick of Aggerhuus, 20 m. NE of Christiania, on the Glommen. The Swedes were defeated near this place, by the Norwegians, in 1809.
BLAKULLEN, an islet of Sweden, in the district of Kalmar, 9 m. NW of Oland and 24 m. from the main, celebrated in Norse legends.

BLAMONT, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Montbeliard. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,356. The com. is 8 m. SSE of Montbeliard. Pop. 679.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Meurthe, arrond. of Luneville. The cant. comprises 31 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,837. The com. and town, on the Vezouze, 18 m. E of Luneville, at an alt. of 800 ft. above sea-level, had a pop. of 2,281 in 1841. There are considerable manufactories of light cottons and woollens here; also tanneries, and agricultural implement manufactories.
BLANC. See BLANCO.

BLANC (MONT), a lofty mountain, the highest of the Pennine Alps, situated in the duchy of Faucigny, a part of the king of Sardinia's dominions, on the borders of Savoy and Piedmont, between 45° 46' and 45° 57' N lat. It receives its name from the immense mantle of snow with which its summit and sides are covered, and which is estimated to extend not less than 7,000 ft., without the least appearance of rock to interrupt its glaring whiteness. General appearance.] When viewed from the Col de Balme and the vale of Chamouny, on the NW, its summit seems to be of a roundish form,—its surface smooth and covered with snow, -its whole appearance uniting beauty with grandeur, and its towering head rising majestically above the surrounding mountains; but, when seen from the valley of Aost, its sides are less completely covered with snow,—its aspect more rugged and dark, - and the prospect which it presents partakes more of the wild and terrific. It rises imperceptibly from amidst the numerous irregular mountains which bound the vale of Chamouny, and, on this side, terminates rather abruptly in a point or top called Aiguille de Gouté, or Dôme de Gouté. Alt. 12,204 ft. Beyond this height, with a considerable hollow between, it forms another mount, called the Middle dome. From this point it gradually sinks again into a concave surface, in the midst of which is a small pyramid of ice, and then shoots up to its highest point of elevation, in the shape of a compressed hemisphere, called from its form as seen from the NE, La Bosse de Dromedaire. Upon nearer inspection, the summit of this gigantic mountain is found to be a narrow ridge, lying nearly in a horizontal direction, and resembling the roof of a house; and at its W end, scarcely sufficiently broad to admit of two persons walking abreast. The snow, which covers the top, is encrusted with ice of a firm consistence, but penetrable by a staff; and beneath this icy surface, especially on the declivities of the summit, is discovered a soft dusty snow without any cohesion. Structure.] The highest rocks of Mont Blanc are formed of strata of granite, running parallel to one another, and nearly in a vertical direction. Those on the E side are mixed with steatites; those on the S with schoerl and lapis corneus; and some of them, about 150 yards below the summit, have the appearance of having been shivered with lightning. The entire mass stretches, in a direction from SW to NE, nearly 13 m., with a breadth varying from 5 m. to 6 m.; or taking the chain of which it forms the culminating point, Mont B. and its adjacent tributaries form a kind of oval group rather than a continuous line, extending from the Col du Bonhomme in the Tarentaise, to Mont Catogne above Martigny, a right angles to this length, from Chamouny to Courmayeur, of 13 m. [Forbes.] Its geographical position, as determined by the French engineers, is N lat. 45° 49′ 58".84 N, E long of Paris 4° 31' 42".52, of Greenwich 6° 52' 6".5. The buttresses, or outlying flanks of Mont B., are all composed of calcareous strata turned up against the granitic mass. Less precipitous than the rest of the front they afford a footing for trees, differing in species according to height, and ascending to from 3,000 to 4,000 ft. above Chamouny. Those of the first zone are deciduous; to them succeed pines; and then follow larches. The interval between each of these verdant buttresses is filled up by a glacier. Height.] Mont Blanc is unquestionably the highest mountain in Europe. According to De Luc, its height above the level of the sea is 2,391} French toises, or 15,304 English ft.; according to Sir George Shuckburgh, 15,662 ft.; and according to other observations, 15,680 ft. above the level of the sea. De Saussure estimated it at 14,700 French or 15,664 English ft. M. Roger, an officer of engineers in the service of the Swiss confederation, estimated the height of the mountain to be 14,542 ft. above the lake of Geneva, which is 1,233 ft. above sea-level, making the total alt. 15,775 ft. The Baron de Zach, taking the mean of several trigonometrical measurements, estimates it at 2,462 toises, or 15,743 ft. These measurements are as follows: Toises. ... by French engineers, from Mont Grenier, 2460-1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Mean, Mont Rosa, which ranks next to it, is 2,366 toises, or 96 toises = 614 ft. lower. Though inferior to Chimborazo in its elevation above the sea, Mont B. rises 12,300 ft. above the valley of Chamouny; while Chimborazo only reaches 11,600 ft. above the plain of Quito. The line of perpetual snow on Mont B. is nearly 7,000 ft. below the summit; on Chimborazo it is only 2,400 ft. Observations on its summit.] At the elevation of 11,392 ft. above the sea, M. De Saussure observed the Silene acaulis, or moss campion in flower; still higher, on the most elevated rocks, he found the Lichen sulphureus and L. rupestris of Hoffman; and, on the summit, he noticed two butterflies on the wing, which he supposed to have been driven thither by the wind. On the top of Mont Blanc, on the 3d of August, Reaumur's thermometer stood, in the shade, at <math>2\frac{3}{10}</math> below the freezing point, or <math>27^{\circ}</math> of Fahrenheit; while, at the same time, at Geneva, it was found 22.6 or 82° of Fahrenheit; which gives a difference of nearly 25° of Reaumur, or 45° of Fahrenheit, between the temperature of the atmosphere at both places. De Luc's barometer fell to 16.01440, while it stood at Geneva at 27.21085,—a difference of 11.2, with a small fraction. By experiments with the hygrometer, the air on the summit of Mont B. was found to contain six times less humidity than that of Geneva; and to this extreme dryness of the atmosphere, the burning thirst which Saussure, and all who have ascended this mountain, experienced, is supposed to have been owing. While 15 or 16 minutes are sufficient to boil water at Geneva, and 14 or 15 minutes at the sea-side, it requires half-an-hour on the top of Mont B. De Saussure found the boiling point of water to be 68° 993 of Reaumur, or 187° 234 of Fahrenheit. By experiments with the electrometer, the electricity of the air was found to be posidistance of 30 m.; with a breadth upon a line at | tive, and the balls diverged only three lines; and by experiments with lime water, and the caustic alkali, atmospheric acid or fixed air was detected in the atmosphere. Owing to the extreme rarefaction of the air, sounds were remarkably feeble: the report of a pistol discharged on the summit did not exceed that of a small Chinese cracker in a room. From the same cause, respiration becomes exceedingly difficult at so immense an altitude; and it was found, that the pulses of three persons, which beat at Chamouny, in a state of repose, 49, 60, and 72, were increased, on the summit of Mont B., to 98, 112, and 100. Luminous appearance.] Many travellers have noticed the exquisitely beautiful effect produced by the varying colours exhibited by the summits of the Alps, and more especially of Mont B. during and after sunset. Goethe thus describes an eveningapproach to the vale of Chamouny, from Geneva: "It became darker . . . only the greater masses were visible. The stars one after another rose, and we observed, over the summits of the mountains, a light which we could not understand: clear without lustre, like the Milky way, but denser,—almost like the Pleiades, but greater,-it long fixed our attention, until at last, as we changed our position, like a pyramid pervaded by an inward mysterious light, it projected over the tops of all the mountains, and we became certain that it was the summit of Mont B. The beauty of this sight was most extraordinary; for, as it shone with the stars that stood around it,-not indeed in an equally intense light, yet with a broader more connected mass, it appeared to our eyes to belong to a higher sphere, and it required an effort to conceive its base fixed to our earth." Professor Necker of Geneva, in a letter to Sir D. Brewster, thus more soberly describes this phenomenon, and accounts for it: "When the sun is near setting, and the weather is serene, all the mountains of the Alps, facing the W, are tinged with a fine purplish hue, which on Mont B., on account of its bright covering of snow, takes a tinge more verging towards a light orange. When the sun has set for the plain, these mountains appear more vivid and more illuminated, by the effect of contrast. When, some minutes after, the lower mountains are in the shade, their purple hue is changed into a dark bluish tinge, the contrast between their shaded parts and those that were lighted by the sun has disappeared, and an almost uniform greyish blue shade covers them all; at this time Mont B. remains the only terrestrial object still lighted by the rays of the sun, and that circumstance causes its immense mass of snow to appear more bright, and its yellowish orange colour more vivid: at the same time the contrast between the projected and other shadows and the lighted parts is at its maximum (I have two or three times seen Mont B. at that moment, and when dark clouds were behind it, look almost as bright and red as a live coal). When, however, the sun has set for Mont B.,-which happens about a quarter of an hour after it has set for the plain round Geneva,then the whole of Mont B. assumes a dull bluish white hue, and a flattened appearance, owing to the absence of contrast from the once shaded parts with those that were lighted. And so its new aspect is to that which it offered a few minutes before, like that of a dead body to a living and healthy one. This pale, and as it were morbid appearance of the mountain, is owing to the fact, that above it exists still a wide zone of atmosphere loaded with thin and light vapours, for which the sun has not yet set, and which on that account are still lighted vividly, and coloured with a purple hue. When, however, the sun has also been setting for these higher regions of the atmosphere, the contrast between their illumination and the shade existing all over Mont B., to which was owing that bluish and deadly colour assumed by the eternal snows, having ceased to take place, the Mont B. assumes once more, but in a much fainter and darker manner, its orange yellow colour; and the lower and nearer mountains recover their purplish hue. All the objects then being uniformly and altogether illuminated by the much paler and less powerful light of the twilight, as they were before all lighted at once by the brighter, but equally uniformly spread, light of the sun; so that everything being placed in the same relative quantity and quality of illumination as before, an analogous aspect is seen in both cases, though much darker under the latter than under the former circumstances. Hence it appears to me that the whole of the phenomenon is most naturally and easily explained by contrast." Attempts to reach its summit.] The ascent of this mountain is particularly hazardous and toilsome; and in consequence of repeated failures on the part of those who made the attempt, was for a long time deemed utterly impracticable. A short sketch of the earlier of these adventurous excursions may not be uninteresting to our readers; and may enable them-better than any description can do-to form a conception of the amazing height and wintry horrors of Mont Blanc. The first recorded attempt was made by M. Couteran, and three guides of Chamouny. They set out from the priory-which has an alt. of 3,425 ft. above sealevel-about 11 P. M. on the 13th of July, 1776; and after spending 14 hours in surmounting many dangerous ascents, crossing valleys of ice, and traversing plains of snow, reached the summit next to Mont Blanc, and about 13,000 ft. above the Mediterranean. Here they at first imagined themselves to be within a league of its summit; but they soon perceived that it would require other four hours to reach it; and as the day was far advanced, and the vapours gathering into clouds, they were obliged to relinquish their enterprise; and, after a journey of 22 hours, arrived at Chamouny about 8 P. M.—The indefatigable Bourrit next excited a spirit of enterprise among the inhabitants of Chamouny. After repeated unsuccessful attempts, he departed from Bionasay on the 11th Sept., 1784, accompanied by six guides, and was scaling, as he expressed it, the rampart of Mont Blanc, when he became so extremely affected by the intensity of the cold, as to be unable to proceed. Two of his guides, having gone before the company, ascended to the Dome de Gouté, passed the Middle dome, and walked along the ridge between it and the summit, as far as some high rocks which appear from the vale of Chamouny like small points in the snow; but the approach of night compelled them to return.—On the 13th Sept., 1785, Messieurs. de Saussure and Bourrit, with twelve guides, left Bionasay, passed the night at a hut which they had ordered to be constructed about 7,808 ft. above the level of the sea, and reached the Dome de Gouté next morning without much difficulty; but a fresh fall of snow rendered farther progress impracticable. In July 1786, six guides of Chamouny failed in another attempt; but Jacques Balmat, one of their number, having separated from his companions, passed the night in a spot above the Dome de Gouté, more than 12,000 ft. above the level of the sea; and, having reconnoitred the situation next morning, observed a place of more easy access than any that had hitherto been attempted. On his return to Chamouny, he communicated his observation to Dr. Paccard, a physician of the place, and engaged to conduct him to the summit of the mountain. They set out from Chamouny on the 7th of August, spent the night on the mountain of La Cote; at three in the morning pursued their route to the Dome de Gouté; passed under the Middle dome towards the E, along the ridge which is seen from Geneva, and which lies on the l. of the summit. Here Dr. Paccard was almost deterred by the cold and fatigue from pursuing the enterprise; but encouraged by Balmat to proceed, and frequently walking sideways to shelter their faces from the piercing wind, they at length, about 6 P. M., and after an ascent of 15 hours, attained the summit of Mont Blanc. They remained about halfan-hour on a spot, which no one before them had been able to reach, and when the cold was so intense as to freeze the provisions in their pockets, congeal the ink in their inkstands, and sink the mercury of Fahrenheit's thermometer to 183°; their faces were excoriated, their lips swelled, and their sight greatly debilitated by the reflection of the snow.—On the 13th August, 1787, M. de Saussure set out from Chamouny, accompanied by 18 guides, and provided with a tent, mattrasses, philosophical instruments, and all necessary accommodations. They passed the first night on the top of La Cote; encamped at 4 o'clock in the following afternoon, about 12,762 ft. above the level of the sea; and next morning pursued the ascent, in places frequently so steep that the guides were obliged to hew out steps with a hatchet, They reached the summit about 11 o'clock in the forenoon, where they remained 41 hours, enjoying a most sublime prospect, and making a variety of observations and experiments. The colour of the sky was dark blue, and the stars were visible in the shade. The therm, indicated 26% in the shade, and 29° in the sun; while at Geneva it was 87°. They experienced great difficulty of respiration, which was increased by the slightest exertion, by a stooping posture, and by the use of wine or brandy while on the summit; and felt no appetite for food, nor relief in anything but in draughts of fresh water. About 2 P. M. they began to descend, and arrived next morning, without accident, at the valley of Chamouny.-On the 8th of August, a few days after Saussure's expedition, Mr. [afterwards Colonel] Beaufoy, an English gentleman, succeeded in a similar attempt; but on account of the enlargement of the chasms in the ice, it was accomplished with greater difficulty. His narrative of this ascent was published in the Annals of Philosophy, No. 50, Feb. 1817. His account of the effect produced upon the mind of the spectator by the view from the top of Mont B. may be quoted. "When the spectator begins to look round him from this elevated height, a confused impression of immensity is the first effect produced upon his mind; but the blue colour, deep almost to blackness, of the canopy above him, soon arrests his attention. He next surveys the mountains, many of which, from the clearness of the air, are to his eye within a stone's throw from him; and even those of Lombardy seem to approach his neighbourhood: while, on the other side, the vale of Chamouny, glittering with the sunbeams, is to the view directly below his feet, and affects his head with giddiness. On the other hand, all objects, of which the distance is great, and the level low, are hid from his eye by the blue vapour which intervenes, and through which I could not discern the lake of Geneva, though, at the height of 15,700 English feet, which, according to Saussure, was the level on which I stood: even the Mediterranean sea must have been within the line of vision. The air was still, and the day so remarkably fine, that I could not discover in any part of the heavens the appearance of a single cloud."—In August 1825, Dr. E. J. Clark, in company with Captain Sherwill, succeeded in ascending Mont B. Dr. Clark's account, as given in a lecture before the Royal institution, will be found in the Quart. Journal of Science and Literature; and Captain Sherwill's in the New Monthly Magazine, vols. xviii. and xix. They reached the summit at about 5 minutes after 3 P. M. of 26th August, and found the barometer mark 15 in. 9.1 lines French. The day before, at Chamouny, it had stood at 25 in. 1.1 line. The therm. at the same moment was a of a degree below Zero of Reaumur. "It being 3 o'clock, or a little later," says Captain Sherwill, "the sun was in the SW, and interrupted our view very much in the direction of Geneva and the Jura: the Jura mountains formed our horizon in that direction. Langres, the highest town in France, we could not discover, although it has been said that the chain of the Alps has been seen from it. On the opposite side of Mont Blanc the Apennines were visible for a vast extent, and the situations of Milan and Turin were pointed out to us. We looked very carefully and with an earnest wish to discover anything like the Mediterranean sea in the line over Genoa, but I cannot say that I saw it. On the side of Switzerland the Jungfrau, near Grindelwald, was very conspicuous, as well as the Mont Buet, the Diablerets, the Gemmi, and St. Gothard; more to the eastward, Monte Rosa was visible, which is 2,430 toises above the level of the sea. The valley of Chamouny, and even its little village, can be seen with a telescope. The convent of the Grand St. Bernard was hid, owing to its situation among the mountains. We could not see much of the Allee Blanche, or the environs of Mont Blanc, immediately at its base on that side, owing to the various projections, which, as it were, grow from its sides. The day was remarkably fine—there was not a cloud above our heads; but on many of the lesser chains of mountains, and on the Apennines, there were light clouds and vapour. Coutet endeavoured to make me see a star; but either he was mistaken, or his eyes were better than mine. I walked to the extreme end of the summit, and looked over towards the Maritime Alps: this chain was very distinct. The length of the summit of Mont Blanc is 200 paces, and nearly level. I found a difficulty in measuring the width, for the sides are an immediate descent, so that I did not know where to begin or where to finish. The whole figure may be understood by the common term, a hog's-back shape.—I am told that the summit of this mountain has been described as forming nearly a triangle; but we did not find this to be the case now. It is evident, from the drifts of snow during the turbulent winds in winter, that its appearance may change in twenty-four hours; and that which is of a triangular form to-day may be circular to-morrow. The variety and changes in the drifts of snow on exposed plains, are sufficiently well known to every traveller. Every object seen from this summit becomes so diminutive, with the exception of a few principal mountains, and so fore-shortened, that it seems entirely to have changed its form and character. The beautiful Aiguille du Midi, and those of Charmoz,—the delight and admiration of every stranger who visits Chamouny,—are in some degree lost in the general confusion; I ought to say, in truth, that no one particular object could be seen distinctly: everything appears so massed together, that all power of distinction is lost. While stationary on the summit of Mont Blanc, I experienced a very peculiar sensation of lightness of body, which was also felt by some of the party, to whom I mentioned the circumstance. It appeared as if I could have passed the blade of a knife under the sole of my shoes, or between them and the ice on which I stood. I mentioned this fact to Dr. Ebel at Zurich, and also to other persons whom I thought capable of elucidating the cause; but their explanations were not satisfactory. The subject will, perhaps, serve to amuse your studious hours: I only vouch for the fact. The rarity of the air did not affect me quite so much on the summit, as when labouring through the deep snows and climbing the ascents on our passage, during the ten hours we were employed in ascending from Les Grands Mulets. The sense of hearing was not interrupted, if there had been anything to hear; the smell, and power of feeling, were not diminished; but the faculties of the mind were in less activity. The sky was of a very dark indigo blue; this deep tint was, indeed, among the most remarkable features we observed. Being, as we were, above all vapour and thick atmosphere, which rises to a certain height above the level of the earth, the medium through which we saw it was of course more pure; and we looked, as it were, into a dark ocean of infinite space. After having well observed, as far as could be distinguished, the principal objects within reach, and again examined our glasses, the barometer was found to remain steady: the thermometer, however, had somewhat changed, and the mercury had fallen a very little below the point at which it was first marked, viz., three-fourths of a degree below zero of Reaumur. The wind began to freshen from the SW, and Coutet would not suffer us to remain. The snow drifted from the neighbouring mountains, and was hurrying along the surface of the summit about half-way up to our knees, but none of it remained on the ice where we stood. This drifting snow, meeting with resistance, such as the body or legs of a man, would soon accumulate, not greatly to his comfort." On the 8th and 9th of August, 1827, Mr. Auldjo achieved the ascent of Mont B., and in the following year he published his Narrative, in 4to. On the 16th and 18th of September, 1834, Dr. Martin Barry effected the ascent of Mont B., being the 16th recorded ascent up to that date. His account is given in the Edinburgh New Phil. Journ., No. xxxv. He found the height of the barometer, at 2½ P. M., to be 17.052 inches; the attached therm. indicating 32°, suspended and screened by the encasing wood from the sun's rays, with a breeze blowing from the S.—Coxe's Travels in Switzerland.—Saussure's Voyages dans les Alpes. Vol. iv.—Martyn's Sketch of a Tour through Switzerland.-Simond's Travels in Switzerland.—Narrative of an Ascent to the Summit of Mont B. By John Auldjo, Esq. London, 1828, 4to. Cambridge, 2d edition, 8vo, 1830 .- Professor Forbes' Travels through the Alps. Edin., 1843.

BLANC (LE), an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of L'Indre.—The arrond. has an area of 187,741 hectares; and comprises the six cant. of Belabre, Saint-Benoist-du- BLA Sault, Le B., Saint-Gaultier, Mezieres-en-Brenne, and Tournon-Saint-Martin. Pop. in 1836, 57,789; in 1846, 59,771.—The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,213.—The com. and town are situated on the Creuse, 38 m. WSW of Chateauroux. Pop. in 1836, 5,095; in 1846, 6,020. There are wool manufactories and tanneries here; and iron is wrought in the environs.
BLANCA, a small town of Spain, in the prov. and 8 m. SSE of Toledo, on the road to Almonacid.

BLANCA, or Casablanca, a small town of Chili, on the road from Valparaiso to Santiago. It is famous for its butter, and other dairy and rural produce, which is collected here, from the neighbouring districts, for transmission either to Valparaiso, 30 m., or Santiago, 40 m. The road from this place across the Andes to Santiago is carried up the Cuesta de Zapata in sixteen zigzag terraces, and, in some places, "reminds one of the very finest parts of the Apennines."—Graham.

BLANCA, an islet off the N part of the coast of Chili, in S lat. 25° 30′, lying in a bay of the same name between Punta Taltal and Punta San Pedro.— Also a lake of La Plata, in the prov. of Santa Fe, near the l. bank of the Rio Salado, in S lat. 30° 20'.

BLANCA, or BLANGUILLA, an island off the coast of Columbia, 71 m. W of the northernmost of the Hermanos islets. It is flat and sterile; 6 m. in length from N to S, and 3 m. from E to W. Its N point is in N lat. 11° 54′ 30″, W long. 64° 41′ 50″.
BLANCAFORT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Argent. Pop. 1,219.

BLANCHARD, a village in Piscatiquis co., in the state of Maine, U.S., 73 m. N by E of Augusta. -Also a township in Hardin co., in Ohio, on Blanchard's fork. Pop. 241.—Also a village in Putnam co., in Ohio. Pop. 688.
BLANCHARD'S BRIDGE, a village in Hancock co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 80 m. N by W of Columbus. Pop. 629.
BLANCHARD'S FORK, the E branch of the Auglaize river, in Ohio.
BLANCHARDSTOWN, a village in co. Dublin, 5 m. NW of Dublin. Pop. 182.

BLANCHE, a river of Lower Canada, in the township of Lochaber, composed of three branches which unite about 1 m. above its junction with the Ottawa. - Also a river of Lower Canada, which joins the NE branch of the Rivière Rouge.
BLANCHE ALLE'E. See ALLE'E BLANCHE.

BLANCHE (GRANDE RIVIERE), a river of Lower Canada, in Ottawa co., which rises far in the interior, and flows into an arm of the Ottawa, after a course of about 100 m. It is navigable for bateaux, in spring, about 15 m.
BLANCHE (Petite Riviere), a river of Lower Canada, which flows into the Ottawa at the SW extremity of Buckingham township.
BLANCHEROCHE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Doubs, com. of Charquemont. Pop. 454. There are glass-works here.
BLANCHESTER, a village in Clinton co., in Ohio, U. S., 80 m. N by W of Augusta.
BLANCHLAND (HIGH), a chapelry in the p. of Shotley, Northumberland, 9 m. S by E of Hexham, on the N bank of the Derwent. Pop. in 1811, 518; in 1841, 476. Lead occurs in the vicinity.
